topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, a suffocating blanket that made my shoulders tense. I was up there looking for old tax documents, and instead, my hand brushed against a brittle, folded piece of paper. It was a letter. My father’s handwriting, elegant and looping, the ink faded to a sepia ghost. A resignation. Decades ago. A low, irritated sound escaped my throat, a barely audible puff of air. I smoothed the paper out on a trunk lid, the texture rough beneath my fingertips. The contents detailed a departure from a firm I barely remembered, a formal announcement wrapped in stiff, bygone formality. I folded it back up, the movement sharp and impatient. Why was this here? It offered no comfort, only a strange, heavy sense of misplaced obligation. I shoved it back into the box, the lid slamming shut with unnecessary force, and started climbing down, eager to be anywhere else.

[story 2]

I was wrestling with a stubborn jar of pickled onions when I stumbled upon it. Tucked beneath a stack of yellowed newspapers was a document. It was my mother’s script, precise and formal, detailing a resignation from a position she’d held long before I was born. A slow, tight knot formed in my chest. I stared at the paper, the lines of text swimming slightly. It felt like an unwelcome intrusion, a relic from a life I didn't fully comprehend. A deep sigh escaped me, the sound heavy with weariness. I ran a thumb over a faded signature, the paper crackling faintly. There was nothing exciting about it, just the cold, hard fact of a life concluded. I didn't want to dwell on it; it was just another piece of dead history demanding attention. I dropped the letter into a nearby wastebasket without a second glance, the action swift and dismissive.

[story 3]

The afternoon light filtering through the grimy window did little to lift the oppressive weight in my stomach. I was supposed to be organizing the garage, a task that demanded a certain level of forced productivity. Instead, I found the envelope tucked inside an old gardening manual. It was a letter from my father, a formal statement of leaving a career, decades past. My jaw tightened as I read the neat, antiquated prose. The words seemed alien, disconnected from the current dull reality I inhabited. A flicker of annoyance crossed my face, a tightening around the mouth. It was absurd, this relic of past ambition. I crumpled the paper slightly, the creases sharp under my thumb, before letting it fall onto the concrete floor. I didn't pick it up again. The silence in the room felt loud, demanding attention, and I preferred the quiet emptiness.
